text: The surgeon opens the omental bursa widely and performs sharp dissection to separate adhesions between the transverse mesocolon and the stomach or the pedicle of the right gastroepiploic vessels. The right gastroepiploic vein is identified, and boundaries of the right gastroepiploic vessels, greater omentum, and transverse mesocolon are delineated. The greater omentum is dissected, but further mobilization of the transverse mesocolon is required as the superior right colic vein is expected to be nearby.

The surgeon isolates and divides the superior right colic vein at its origin, noting the presence of two SRCVs in this patient. The transverse mesocolon is fully mobilized. The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and medial colic vein are exposed and divided at their origins. The duodenum and pancreas are visible. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and middle colic artery are divided, followed by the division of the ileocolic artery.

The surgeon divides the ileocolic vein and continues lymph node dissection. The transverse colon is raised ventrally, and the mesocolon, including the pedicles, is separated from the lateral peritoneal tissues using a medial to lateral approach. The duodenum is visible in the surgical field.

A gauze is inserted into the dissected space, serving as a landmark for the dissection of the lateral attachment of the ascending colon. The mobilization of the right colon is completed, revealing the operator's view after lymph node dissection, showing the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), right gastroepiploic vein, and gastrocolic trunk. Extracorporeal anastomosis is performed.
